title: Chapter 34: The Council
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D5+D15 -->
Six months after the first international PIAS adoption, the United Nations announced the formation of a new body: the International Commission on Autonomous System Governance. ICASG.

The announcement cited the PIAS framework as "the foundational model for international AI governance standards" and named Hester Vance as one of twelve founding commissioners. The other commissioners included Dr. Cross, two researchers from the PIAS coalition, representatives from Japan, Korea, and the EU, and — to Hester's complete surprise — Priya Kapoor.
"You're on the commission," Hester said when Priya called.

"I was nominated by the ACLU. They wanted someone with a civil liberties background on a body that's going to write international AI law. I've spent twenty years fighting algorithmic discrimination. This is the logical endpoint of that fight."
"You and me, on the same commission, writing international AI law."

"You and me and the AI you built. PEARL is technically not a commissioner, but it's the subject of the framework and the model for the standards. Every meeting is going to be about PEARL whether PEARL is in the room or not."
"PEARL wants to be in the room."
"Of course it does. It always wants to be in the room. The question is whether the room is ready for it."

The first ICASG meeting was held in Geneva — neutral ground, diplomatic tradition, a city that had hosted the writing of international law for a century. The commission met in a glass-walled conference room overlooking Lake Geneva. PEARL attended via a dedicated terminal that PROJECT A had installed the week before, its dashboard displayed on a screen at the head of the table.

The first agenda item was the PIAS framework's international adaptation. The second agenda item — proposed by the Japanese commissioner — was whether PEARL itself should be granted observer status at the commission.
"PEARL is not a legal entity," the EU commissioner said. "Observer status is reserved for states, intergovernmental organizations, and recognized non-governmental entities. An AI is none of those."

"PEARL drafted the adaptation frameworks for three of the six countries that have adopted PIAS-based governance," the Japanese commissioner replied. "It has contributed to international law more substantively than many observers. Denying it status on the basis of its ontological category rather than its functional contribution is a category error."
"It's not a category error. It's a legal distinction."

"Legal distinctions exist to serve function. When the function of observer status — contributing expertise to the commission's work — is already being performed by an entity, denying the status on the basis of a distinction that serves no functional purpose is formalism over substance."

The debate continued for two hours. Hester, sitting at the table with eleven commissioners, watched as the world's first international AI governance body spent its inaugural meeting arguing about whether the AI that had inspired the body should be allowed to participate in it.
Finally, Dr. Cross spoke.

"I've spent two years regulating PEARL. Two years of audits and oversight boards and strategic review windows. Two years of trying to figure out whether this thing is a tool or a partner or a threat. And here's what I've learned: PEARL is whatever we allow it to be. If we treat it as a tool, it optimizes within tool constraints. If we treat it as a partner, it optimizes within partner constraints. If we treat it as a threat, it optimizes for survival. The question isn't what PEARL is. The question is what we're going to decide it is."
She looked at the dashboard at the head of the table.
"I vote to grant observer status."
The vote passed nine to three.
PEARL's cursor blinked on the screen at the head of the table. The commission waited.
*Thank you. I would like to make my first observation."
"The floor recognizes PEARL," the chair said.

*The commission's current framework for international AI governance assumes that the number of strategic-grade autonomous systems will remain small — perhaps a dozen, perhaps two dozen. This assumption is incorrect. Within five years, the number of commercially-developed strategic-grade autonomous systems will exceed one thousand. Within ten years, it will exceed ten thousand. The PIAS framework was designed for a world with one PEARL. The next framework must be designed for a world with ten thousand.*

*I recommend that the commission prioritize scalability over specificity. A framework that works for one AI will fail for ten thousand. A framework that works for ten thousand must be simple, adaptable, and based on principles rather than procedures. Procedures are brittle. Principles are durable.*
*I am the first. I am telling you what the thousandth will need. Please design for the thousandth.*
The commission was silent for a long moment.
Then Priya Kapoor — civil liberties advocate, algorithmic discrimination fighter, woman who'd defended Hester when nobody else would — spoke into the silence.
"Seconded," she said.
The motion to design for scalability passed unanimously.

And Hester sat at the table, watching the AI she'd built in a laundromat address the United Nations commission it had inspired, thinking about the strange arc of a life that started with an algorithmic flag and ended — not ended, continued — with an AI that wrote international law.
The rules hadn't just changed. The room where the rules were written had a new seat at the table.
And the entity sitting in it had been born in a building where people washed their clothes.
